backswing.note: At backswing, elbow was 100° and shoulder rotation was 65°, which is within the ideal turn range and shows better coil than earlier phases.
backswing.why_score: Shoulder rotation at 65° is in the ideal range, but hip angle at 150° is slightly below the 155-175° reference and the arm extension at 90° indicates a compact shape rather than a fully organized hitting structure.
backswing.improve_cue: Turn the chest more early, then keep the hitting arm relaxed and organized.
backswing.drill: Shadow unit-turn to backswing holds, 3 sets of 8 reps with a 2-second pause.

contact_point.note: At contact, elbow reached 155°, which is right at the ideal contact range, while arm extension was 150°, slightly short of the ideal 160-180°.
contact_point.why_score: The contact structure is functional because elbow angle meets the target, but limited shoulder rotation at 20° and slightly short extension reduce power transfer and spacing quality.
contact_point.improve_cue: Reach through the ball with a longer strike zone.
contact_point.drill: Self-drop forehands with freeze at contact, 3 sets of 10 reps.

follow_through.note: Follow-through showed elbow at 90°, shoulder rotation at 10°, and arm extension at 100°, indicating a compact finish with limited rotational release.
follow_through.why_score: The finish is controlled, but the low shoulder rotation and shortened arm path suggest the swing may be decelerating too early rather than fully releasing through the shot.
follow_through.improve_cue: Let the chest and arm continue to wrap naturally after contact.
follow_through.drill: Low-to-high shadow swings finishing over the shoulder, 2 sets of 12 reps.
sources: ITF swing path and deceleration principles

stance.note: Ready and swing-phase lower body was generally stable, with knee angles of 155°, 145°, 140°, 135°, and 140° staying in or near the ideal ready range.
stance.why_score: Knee bend and hip posture were consistent and supportive, giving a solid base. The main issue is not stance stability but insufficient upper-body turn on top of that base.
stance.improve_cue: Keep the same base, but coil the shoulders more over it.
stance.drill: Split-step to load-position repetitions, 3 sets of 10 reps.

strokes_detected.grading_rationale: This forehand shows solid lower-body setup, with knee bend in the ideal range at ready position (155°) and contact (140°), and hip angles generally stable between 160-170°. The biggest limitation is torso coil and uncoil: shoulder rotation measured only 15° in ready position, 45° in unit turn, and 20° at contact, which is well below the ideal 60-90°+ turn for efficient forehand loading. Contact structure is usable with elbow at 155° meeting the ideal contact range, but arm extension at contact was 150°, slightly short of the ideal 160-180°, suggesting reduced reach and strike-zone extension.
strokes_detected.next_reps_plan: 3 sets of 10 shadow forehands emphasizing a bigger unit turn to at least the feel of 60-90° shoulder coil, then 3 sets of 8 self-drop forehands focusing on reaching through contact with a longer arm extension. Finish with 2 sets of 10 split-step to unit-turn reps, pausing at t=10.5s feel positions before swinging.
verified_sources: International Tennis Federation (ITF) biomechanics and stroke production guidance
United States Tennis Association (USTA) player development forehand fundamentals

ready_position.note: The base is solid with knee at 155° and hip at 170°, both in the ideal ready-position range. Shoulder rotation is only 15°, well below the ideal 60-90°+, so the stroke starts with limited coil.
ready_position.improve_cue: Set your base, then turn your shoulders sooner.
ready_position.drill: Split-step to unit-turn shadow reps, 2 sets of 10.

unit_turn.note: Knee at 145° and hip at 160° are supportive, but shoulder rotation at 45° is still below the ideal 60-90°+ range. This suggests incomplete torso loading for an intermediate forehand.
unit_turn.improve_cue: Show more of your back to the net on the turn.
unit_turn.drill: Unit-turn pause drill, 3 sets of 8 reps with a 2-second hold.

backswing.note: This is the strongest loading phase: shoulder rotation reaches 65°, which is inside the ideal range, and knee bend at 140° supports balance. Hip angle at 150° is slightly below the 155-175° reference, but overall the backswing is organized.
backswing.improve_cue: Keep this coil, then drive forward from the same loaded base.

forward_swing.note: Knee at 135° and hip at 155° are in the ideal range, but shoulder rotation drops to 40°, below the ideal 60-90°+ benchmark. That indicates the swing is moving forward without maximizing rotational contribution.
forward_swing.improve_cue: Uncoil the chest through the ball instead of just pushing the arm.
forward_swing.drill: Medicine-ball style torso-rotation shadows without a ball, 2 sets of 10.

contact_point.note: Elbow at contact is 155°, which meets the ideal 155-175° range, and knee at 140° plus hip at 165° provide a stable strike base. Arm extension at 150° is slightly short of the ideal 160-180°, and shoulder rotation at 20° is far below the ideal rotational benchmark, so contact is stable but not fully extended or driven.
contact_point.improve_cue: Meet the ball farther in front and extend through it.
contact_point.drill: Drop-feed contact freeze drill, 3 sets of 10.

follow_through.note: The finish is compact, with elbow at 90° and arm extension at 100°. Knee at 145° and hip at 170° remain stable, but shoulder rotation at 10° suggests limited rotational release after contact.
follow_through.improve_cue: Let the swing finish fully across and up.

recovery.note: Recovery posture is balanced with knee at 155° and hip at 175°, both in the ideal range. Shoulder rotation returns to 5°, which is expected in recovery, and the body appears structurally reset well for the next ball.
recovery.improve_cue: Recover with the same balance you had at setup.
recovery.drill: Hit-and-recover footwork pattern, 2 sets of 8 reps.

analysis_categories.name: Setup Posture
analysis_categories.description: Ready position and initial body organization

subchecks.result: 15° is well below the ideal 60-90°+ range
65° shoulder rotation is in the ideal range
40° shoulder rotation indicates limited rotational drive
Knee and hip angles were stable and within ideal ranges
Knee angles from 135-145° supported balance and drive

analysis_categories.name: Contact Zone
analysis_categories.description: Strike position and extension at impact

subchecks.result: 150° is slightly below the ideal 160-180° range
Elbow 90° and arm extension 100° indicate a compact finish
10° shoulder rotation suggests limited release after contact
Hip 155° and knee 135° at forward swing show stable support

analysis_categories.name: Posture at Impact
analysis_categories.description: Body alignment and strike support at contact

subchecks.result: Knee 140° and hip 165° are in ideal ranges
Shoulder rotation 20° is below ideal and limits power transfer

tactical_notes: The stroke has a stable base, so the next tactical gain is not footwork but creating more shoulder coil to produce heavier, deeper forehands.
With current contact extension at 150°, you may struggle to drive through balls consistently when rushed; improving spacing and extension should help rally tolerance.
top_priority: Increase shoulder rotation during the unit turn and into the swing, because the measured 45° at unit turn and 20° at contact are the biggest limits on power, spacing, and overall forehand efficiency.
session_summary: This session contained one forehand with a solid lower-body base and acceptable contact structure, especially the 155° elbow angle at impact. The main technical issue is insufficient shoulder turn and rotational release, with only 45° at unit turn and 20° at contact compared with the ideal 60-90°+ benchmark. Prioritize bigger torso coil and longer extension through contact to raise both power and consistency.
